Variables and files (inputs and outputs)

Source: vignettes/variables-and-files.Rmd
variables-and-files.Rmd

🇪🇸 Esta página también está disponible en español: Variables y archivos.

This page documents the data model of oraltags: which files the application needs, which files it creates, and all the variables — manually annotated and automatically computed — stored in each analysis.

Input files

File Format Required Notes
Audio .wav, .mp3 Yes MP3 is converted to WAV internally (av package)
Video .mp4 No Enables the video viewer; with ffmpeg the exact segment clip is cut
Transcription .TextGrid (Praat) Yes (first time) If it contains the phones and words tiers (Montreal Forced Aligner), the app generates the intonation groups automatically with a configurable minimum-pause threshold
Transcription .csv / .txt Alternative Must include at least the columns speaker, start, end, label (times in seconds)

Audio and transcription are paired by base name (interview.wav ↔︎ interview.TextGrid).

Output files

All are created under the user data folder (oraltags::oraltags_data_dir() in the package version; the repository folders if you run the source script):

File When it is created Content
analisis/analisis_<name>.txt When generating the IGs or saving the first annotation TSV with one row per intonation group and all the variables (see below)
analisis/analisis_todos.txt Updated on every save Consolidated file of all corpora; adds the filename column with the source file. It feeds Statistics → Full corpus
backup/<name>_backup_<date>.txt When reloading data and before deleting an analysis Timestamped backup copy
config/etiquetas_variables.txt When saving changes in the variable editor Custom scheme (labels and categories of anot1–anot33)
config/preferencias.txt When saving preferences Language, chart font size, encouraging message
audios/<name>.wav When uploading audio (package version) Copy of the audio, reused in future sessions

Analysis files are plain TSV (UTF-8): you can open them in R, Excel or any editor. The app also exports directly to CSV (sidebar button), each table has Copy / CSV / Excel buttons, and charts can be downloaded as PNG (300 dpi) and PDF.

Structure of the analysis file

Each row of analisis_<name>.txt is an intonation group (IG) with these columns:

Identification and text

Column Description
speaker Speaker identifier (from the TextGrid or CSV)
start, end Start and end times in seconds
label Transcription of the segment
contexto Discourse context (speaker: text | …) generated automatically
observaciones Free notes by the annotator
filename Consolidated file only: source file

Acoustic and speech-rate metrics (computed)

Computed automatically while navigating (or in bulk with “Compute F0/Int for all segments”). F0 is estimated with wrassp::ksvF0 and intensity with wrassp/seewave:

Column Description
n_palabras Number of words in the segment
palabras_por_seg Speech rate in words/second
fonemas_por_seg Speech rate in phonemes/second
F0_mean, F0_median, F0_sd Mean, median and standard deviation of F0 (Hz)
F0_range_st p10–p90 pitch range in semitones
F0_delta_st Global inflection of the segment (semitones)
F0_final_delta_st Movement of the toneme (final stretch, 20 % by default) in semitones
F0_final_pattern Melodic pattern of the toneme (sequence of rises/falls/plateaus, e.g. PPP)
Int_mean, Int_median, Int_sd Mean, median and standard deviation of intensity (dB)
F0_ini_q1–F0_ini_q4 Mean F0 in the 4 quarters of the initial window of the segment
F0_fin_q1–F0_fin_q4 Mean F0 in the 4 quarters of the final window
Int_ini_q1–Int_ini_q4, Int_fin_q1–Int_fin_q4 Same for intensity

The size of the initial/final windows is controlled with Settings → Quartile percentage (%) (20 % of the segment by default).

In addition, the report of the Metrics subtab shows the preceding and following pauses (computed from the times of adjacent IGs).

Annotation variables (anot1–anot33)

These are the manual variables of the Annotations form. The following labels and categories are the defaults; all can be modified in Settings → Edit variables (the TSV always stores the text of the category, not a code):

Structure block

Column Variable
anot1 Utterance type (structure)
anot2 Sentence modality
anot3 Information status
anot4 Syntactic complexity
anot5 Reformulation / expansion
anot6 Global discourse function
anot7 Reference to others’ discourse
anot8 Temporality of the content

Pragmatics block

Column Variable
anot9 Basic pragmatic function
anot10 Interpersonal function
anot11 Mitigation: presence and overall type
anot12 Mitigation: main orientation
anot13 Mitigation: dominant procedure
anot14 Intensification
anot15 Politeness strategy
anot16 Other’s face
anot17 Self-image

Discourse and interaction block

Column Variable
anot18 Conversational move
anot19 Turn management
anot20 Relation to the previous turn
anot21 Interactive dynamics (overlap/rhythm)
anot22 Main discourse marker
anot23 Phatic / contact function
anot24 Dominant deixis
anot25 Colloquial resources and fillers

Paralinguistic / non-verbal block

Column Variable
anot26 Paralanguage (non-verbal sounds)
anot28 Non-verbal overlaps
anot29 Audible articulatory / gestural noise
anot30 Breathing phenomena
anot31 Non-verbal sounds as turn-taking
anot32 Ambient noise with discourse impact
anot33 Non-verbal vocal attitude

Emotions block

Column Variable
anot27 Ekman emotional tone + intensity, stored as Emotion - N (e.g. Alegría - 4)

Note on language: although the interface can be displayed in English, categories are always stored with their canonical Spanish text, so that analyses by different annotators (and different interface languages) are directly comparable.

Derived variables in Statistics and Agreement

  • Statistics treats as numeric the duration, the speech rates and all F0/intensity metrics, and as categorical the speaker, the file (filename, in the full corpus) and any anot* with between 2 and 30 observed levels.
  • Agreement matches the segments of 2–10 judges by start/end/label and computes per variable: % agreement, Cohen’s kappa (weighted if the variable is marked as ordinal), Fleiss’ kappa, mean pairwise kappa and Krippendorff’s α (requires irr), with the Landis & Koch interpretation.

On this page
